prompt: Design a ClubOps Governance & Finance Portal to run an offline, multi-site membership organization that delivers services and tracks performance. The English UI is built with HTMX-driven server-rendered screens so users get fast, form-level feedback without heavy client scripting: participants can register with a username and password, sign in, and see only the menus they’re allowed to use; team leads maintain club profiles with an avatar, searchable tags, a short description, and an “Accepting Members” toggle that immediately updates the public recruitment view. Organizers manage member records in a sortable directory with join dates, positions, active/inactive status, and custom fields (for example, “Training Level” or “Background Check On File”), plus ad-hoc grouping for teams or cohorts; bulk import/export supports local CSV files up to 5,000 rows with inline validation and a downloadable error report. When services are fulfilled, participants can leave order-level reviews with a 1–5 star rating, controlled tags, up to 500 characters of text, and up to five JPG/PNG images (2 MB each) stored locally; reviewers can appeal a hidden review within 7 days, and moderators can hide or reinstate content with a required reason. Finance users set monthly or quarterly budgets by account, campus, and project, view real-time execution rates and projected end-of-period balances, and see on-screen threshold alerts at 85% consumed; budget adjustments over 10% require an approval workflow between an organizer and an administrator. The backend uses Go with Fiber to enforce validation, permissions, and auditability, while SQLite persists all data locally for fully offline operation. Authentication is local-only (no email, SMS, or third-party login): passwords must be at least 12 characters, are hashed with bcrypt, sessions expire after 30 minutes of inactivity, and accounts lock for 15 minutes after 5 failed attempts; account recovery is handled via administrator-initiated reset with a forced password change at next login, and administrators must rotate passwords every 180 days. RBAC applies to pages, Fiber routes, and data scope (for example, a team lead can only see their own club’s members and budgets), and every moderation action, status change, budget approval, and rule update is written to an append-only operational audit log retained for 2 years. Master Data Management maintains product, customer, channel, region, and time dimensions plus sales fact tables, with coding rules (such as fixed-length alphanumeric codes), uniqueness checks, and referential integrity; region hierarchies are editable (for example, state to county to city) and changes are versioned. A credit rule engine supports versioned composition weights, thresholds, makeup/retake logic, and bonus/deduction rules with effective date ranges; calculations always use the rule version active on the transaction date and previously issued credits never recalculate retroactively. Frontend and backend collaborate through REST-like endpoints that return full pages or HTMX partials, with local file storage referenced from SQLite and sensitive fields (such as personal identifiers) encrypted at the application layer using a server-held key. Canary releases are implemented as local feature flags that can be enabled for specific roles or a configured percentage of accounts, with all toggles tracked in the audit log.
